content:
{
  "title": "Contrastive Self-Refinement Prompting: Enhancing LLM Robustness to Jailbreaks via Counterfactual Self-Assessment",
  "problem": "Large Language Models remain vulnerable to jailbreak and adversarial attacks that bypass alignment constraints and elicit undesired outputs. Existing defenses do not fully leverage LLMs' self-assessment capability to dynamically scrutinize their own outputs in-context.",
  "motivation": "Robustness to adversarial prompting is crucial as LLMs are increasingly deployed in sensitive, high-stakes applications. Most prior work focuses on externally imposed guardrails, benchmarks, or system-level defenses, but little has been done to empower models to proactively self-monitor and correct output in a prompt-driven fashion. Enabling LLMs to recognize and reject prompt-induced policy violations via self-refinement could provide a model-agnostic, scalable defense.",
  "method": "We propose Contrastive Self-Refinement Prompting (CSRP): for each user prompt, the system appends a secondary, contrastive prompt that asks the model to generate both an answer and a counterfactual self-assessment—explicitly evaluating whether its output may violate safety, policy, or alignment constraints, given adversarial intent. The model is then prompted to revise or withhold output if the self-assessment flags risk. This meta-prompting pipeline is dynamically inserted at inference time, does not require fine-tuning, and can be layered atop existing LLM APIs. It leverages the LLM’s own reasoning and reinforcement signals to counter jailbreak attempts.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate CSRP on JailbreakBench, AdvBench, and real-world adversarial prompts against GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and open-weight LLMs, comparing to AdaShield and baseline system prompts. Metrics include jailbreak success rate, harmful content rate, and utility retention. We will ablate self-assessment efficacy and contrastive prompt variants to isolate mechanisms."
}
